annotations.label: Fermat's Last Theorem (statement)

anchor.snippet: states that there are no positive integers

annotations.note: FermatLastTheorem is defined as the statement that aⁿ+bⁿ=cⁿ has no nontrivial natural solution for n≥3 (statement only; the full proof is not in Mathlib).

annotations.label: Cases n=1, n=2 have infinitely many solutions

anchor.snippet: have been known since antiquity to have infinitely many solutions

annotations.note: not_fermatLastTheoremFor_one/two exhibit nontrivial solutions for n=1,2 but Mathlib does not state their infinitude.

annotations.label: Pythagorean equation has infinitely many solutions

anchor.snippet: has an infinite number of positive integer solutions for

annotations.note: Mathlib classifies all Pythagorean triples via a parametrization but never states the solution set is infinite.

annotations.label: Fermat's general equation has no solutions for n>2

anchor.snippet: had no solutions in positive integers if

annotations.note: This is exactly the FermatLastTheorem statement (no nontrivial solutions for n≥3).

annotations.label: Reduction to prime exponents

anchor.snippet: is sufficient to establish that if the theorem is false for some exponent

annotations.note: FermatLastTheorem.of_odd_primes (with FermatLastTheoremWith.mono) formalizes that proving FLT for n=4 and odd primes suffices.

annotations.label: Trivial and non-trivial solution

anchor.snippet: is zero a trivial solution

annotations.note: FermatLastTheoremWith encodes non-triviality via the hypotheses a≠0,b≠0,c≠0 but there is no standalone 'trivial solution' definition.

annotations.label: Original statement of FLT

anchor.snippet: are all positive whole numbers

annotations.note: The original positive-integer formulation matches FermatLastTheorem.

annotations.label: Equivalent statement 1 (over the integers)

anchor.snippet: Equivalent statement 1

annotations.note: fermatLastTheoremFor_iff_int proves the natural-number FLT is equivalent to FermatLastTheoremWith ℤ.

annotations.label: Equivalent statement 2 (over the rationals)

anchor.snippet: Equivalent statement 2

annotations.note: fermatLastTheoremFor_iff_rat proves the FLT statement is equivalent to FermatLastTheoremWith ℚ.

annotations.label: Equivalent statement 3 (curve form)

anchor.snippet: Equivalent statement 3

annotations.note: The rational equivalence captures the same content, but Mathlib has no explicit Fermat-curve (xⁿ+yⁿ=1 rational points) formulation.

annotations.label: Equivalent statement 4 (Frey curve / elliptic curves)

anchor.snippet: Equivalent statement 4

annotations.note: The Frey-curve/elliptic-curve reformulation of FLT is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pythagorean theorem

anchor.snippet: This is now known as the Pythagorean theorem

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the Pythagorean theorem in the general inner-product-space form (and an affine/Euclidean version).

annotations.label: Pythagorean triple

anchor.snippet: the definition of a Pythagorean triple is a set of three integers

annotations.note: PythagoreanTriple x y z is defined as x*x+y*y=z*z over ℤ.

annotations.label: Infinitely many Pythagorean triples

anchor.snippet: There are infinitely many such triples

annotations.note: The classification gives a parametrization of all triples but the infinitude of the triple set is not stated as a theorem.

annotations.label: Example Pythagorean triples

anchor.snippet: Examples include (3, 4, 5) and (5, 12, 13)

annotations.note: No declaration asserts specific example triples such as (3,4,5) or (5,12,13).

annotations.label: Diophantine equation

anchor.snippet: is an example of a Diophantine equation

annotations.note: Mathlib defines Diophantine sets (Dioph) for Hilbert's tenth problem, but not a general 'Diophantine equation' object.

annotations.label: Typical Diophantine problem

anchor.snippet: A typical Diophantine problem is to find two integers

annotations.note: This illustrative problem is not formalized as a declaration.

annotations.label: Solutions of x²+y²=z² are Pythagorean triples

anchor.snippet: are given by the Pythagorean triples

annotations.note: PythagoreanTriple.classification gives the explicit parametric form of all integer solutions of x²+y²=z².

annotations.label: Linear Diophantine equation via Euclidean algorithm

anchor.snippet: may be found using the Euclidean algorithm

annotations.note: Bézout's identity Int.gcd_eq_gcd_ab underlies solving linear Diophantine equations, but no general solver/example is stated.

annotations.label: Infinitely many solutions for coprime exponents

anchor.snippet: it is known that there are infinitely many positive integers

annotations.note: This existence-of-infinitely-many result for coprime exponents is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Arithmetica Problem II.8

anchor.snippet: asks how a given square number is split into two other squares

annotations.note: This historical Diophantus problem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Diophantus's sum-of-squares solution for k=4

anchor.snippet: Diophantus shows how to solve this sum-of-squares problem

annotations.note: Diophantus's specific sum-of-squares construction is not formalized.

annotations.label: Fermat's marginal statement of FLT

anchor.snippet: It is impossible to separate a cube into two cubes

annotations.note: Fermat's prose statement is exactly FermatLastTheorem.

annotations.label: Right triangle area not a perfect square

anchor.snippet: the area of a right triangle with integer sides can never equal the square of an integer

annotations.note: Fermat's right-triangle theorem (congruent-number style) is not present; only the related Fermat42 infrastructure exists.

annotations.label: Fermat's Last Theorem for n = 4

anchor.snippet: has no primitive solutions in integers

annotations.note: fermatLastTheoremFour proves FermatLastTheoremFor 4.

annotations.label: Reduction to odd prime exponents

anchor.snippet: has no positive integer solutions ( a , b , c ) when n is an odd prime number

annotations.note: FermatLastTheorem.of_odd_primes proves FLT follows from the odd-prime cases.

annotations.label: FLT for p = 3 (Euler)

anchor.snippet: Leonhard Euler gave a proof of p = 3

annotations.note: fermatLastTheoremThree proves FermatLastTheoremFor 3.

annotations.label: FLT for p = 5 (Legendre, Dirichlet)

anchor.snippet: The case p = 5 was proved

annotations.note: No proof of FermatLastTheoremFor 5 exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: FLT for p = 7 (Lamé)

anchor.snippet: The case p = 7 was proved

annotations.note: No proof of FermatLastTheoremFor 7 exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: FLT for n = 6, 10, 14

anchor.snippet: was also proved for the exponents n = 6, 10, and 14

annotations.note: FermatLastTheoremFor.mono with fermatLastTheoremThree yields n=6 (3∣6), but cases n=10,14 are unavailable since p=5,7 are not proved.

annotations.label: Sophie Germain auxiliary primes

anchor.snippet: she defined a set of auxiliary primes

annotations.note: Sophie Germain's auxiliary primes are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-consecutivity condition implies θ divides xyz

anchor.snippet: then θ must divide the product xyz

annotations.note: This step of Sophie Germain's argument is not formalized.

annotations.label: Sophie Germain's theorem

anchor.snippet: she proved Sophie Germain's theorem

annotations.note: Only Sophie Germain's algebraic identity is in Mathlib; her FLT theorem (first case) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Sophie Germain primes

anchor.snippet: are called Sophie Germain primes

annotations.note: There is no definition of Sophie Germain primes in Mathlib.

annotations.label: First case of FLT for n = 2p (Terjanian)

anchor.snippet: which was proved by Guy Terjanian in 1977

annotations.note: Terjanian's first-case result for exponent 2p is not formalized.

annotations.label: First case of FLT for infinitely many odd primes

anchor.snippet: the first case of Fermat's Last Theorem holds for infinitely many odd primes

annotations.note: This first-case result is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kummer's theorem for regular primes

anchor.section: Ernst Kummer and the theory of ideals
anchor.snippet: Kummer proved both cases of Fermat's Last Theorem for all regular prime numbers

annotations.note: Neither regular primes nor Kummer's FLT theorem are formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Irregular primes below 270

anchor.section: Ernst Kummer and the theory of ideals
anchor.snippet: the only irregular primes below 270 are

annotations.note: Irregular primes are not defined and this enumeration is not present.

annotations.label: Mordell conjecture / Faltings' theorem

anchor.snippet: Fermat's equation has at most a finite number of nontrivial primitive integer solutions

annotations.note: Faltings' theorem / the Mordell conjecture is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: FLT verified up to 2521 (Vandiver)

anchor.snippet: to prove Fermat's Last Theorem for all primes up to 2521

annotations.note: This computational verification range is not formalized.

annotations.label: FLT verified below 125,000 (Wagstaff)

anchor.snippet: had extended this to all primes less than 125,000

annotations.note: This computational verification range is not formalized.

annotations.label: FLT verified below four million

anchor.snippet: had been proved for all primes less than four million

annotations.note: This computational verification range is not formalized.

annotations.label: Modularity theorem

anchor.snippet: states that every elliptic curve is modular

annotations.note: The modularity (Taniyama–Shimura–Weil) theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Frey curve unlikely to be modular

anchor.snippet: would have such unusual properties that it was unlikely to be modular

annotations.note: The Frey curve and its (non-)modularity are not formalized.

annotations.label: Ribet's theorem

anchor.snippet: could be used to create a semistable elliptic curve that could not be modular

annotations.note: Ribet's theorem (epsilon conjecture) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wiles's modularity theorem for semistable curves

anchor.snippet: established the modularity theorem for semistable elliptic curves

annotations.note: Wiles's modularity result for semistable elliptic curves is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: No cube is a sum of two coprime nth powers

anchor.snippet: no cube can be written as a sum of two coprime n th powers

annotations.note: This consequence about cubes as sums of coprime powers is not formalized.

anchor.snippet: considers solutions to the Fermat equation

annotations.note: The Fermat equation aⁿ+bⁿ=cⁿ appears inside FermatLastTheoremWith but is not a standalone definition.

anchor.snippet: states that there are no solutions to the generalized Fermat equation

annotations.note: The Beal conjecture is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fermat–Catalan conjecture

anchor.snippet: has only finitely many solutions

annotations.note: The Fermat–Catalan conjecture is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.note: The inverse Fermat equation and its solutions are not formalized.

anchor.snippet: to the optic equation

annotations.label: No solutions for n < −2

anchor.snippet: then the radical d of abc is usually not much smaller than c

annotations.label: abc conjecture implies FLT for large n

anchor.snippet: implies Fermat's last theorem for n that are sufficiently large

annotations.note: The implication abc ⇒ FLT for large n is not formalized in Mathlib.
